I'm a bit confused about this size parameter. None of the solutions seems to be acting consistently with the rest of the algorithms. In *average_hash* and *dhash* functions the **hash_size** parameter defines length of the one side of the square matrix.

```python
hash = imagehash.average_hash(img, hash_size=8)
print(hash.hash.size, hash.hash.shape)
>> (64, (8, 8))

hash = imagehash.dhash(img, hash_size=8)
print(hash.hash.size, hash.hash.shape)
>> (64, (8, 8))
```

Suggestions by both **Queatz** ```python dctlowfreq = dct[:hash_size/4, 1:hash_size/4+1]``` and **wyverntiger** ```python dctlowfreq = dct[:int(hash_size/4), :int(hash_size/4)]``` produce unexpected sizes.
```python
hash = imagehash.phash(img, hash_size=8)
print(hash.hash.size, hash.hash.shape)
>> (4, (2, 2))
```
Would it be possible for the functions to act consistently? Am I missing the point? Thanks.

Previous, while I tried using `NoneType` in a test. You can test this on the master branch by modifying the `test_update_arg_with_anno` in `test_stubs.py` as follows.

```Python
    def test_update_arg_with_anno(self):
        """Leave existing arg annotations alone"""
        sig = Signature.from_callable(UpdateSignatureHelper.has_annos)
        sig = update_signature_args(sig, {'a': str, 'b': NoneType}, False)
        expected = Signature(
            parameters=[
                Parameter('a', Parameter.POSITIONAL_OR_KEYWORD, annotation=int),
                Parameter('b', Parameter.POSITIONAL_OR_KEYWORD, annotation=NoneType)
            ],
            return_annotation=int
        )
        assert sig == expected
```

I found that with the previous code, when a parameter has a trace suggesting `NoneType`, it will annotate it as `NoneType`. You will pass the test in this way. Thus, I thought that is because we only checked `None` but not `NoneType` in the `update_signature_arg` part, resulting in this behavior. I think as you mentioned in other part, the desire behavior is to return `None` in this way?

So I thought about this a bit more and there are still some problems but also some potential solutions. We don't really know the module name to rewrite it to, since it could be one of: `foo.py`, `foo.__init__.py`, or `foo.__main__.py`, depending on the module.

We could figure it out with some probing of the filesystem, and possibly some other tricks I haven't thought of, but might not be easily workable.

However another option might be to do it entry points style, e.g. something like the explicit `foo.__main__:main`, and instead of using `run_module` we import `main` from `foo.__main__` and run it ourselves.

1. You're going to need two tests here, one for the tabs is non-empty case and one for the empty case (where it raises an exception).

2. I expect this test came from somewhere else in the code base which means I probably wrote its progenitor, but I'm inclined toward a straight-through approach here where one test case calls `tab_stops[idx]` once and only once. That makes the test method as simple and straightforward as possible. If there is merit in it being called more than once on the same XML structure, that can be implemented as multiple cases (params) in the fixture.

  ```python
  def it_can_get_a_tab_stop_by_index(self, index_fixture):
      tab_stops, idx, TabStop_, tab, tab_stop_ = index_fixture

      tab_stop = tab_stops[idx]

      TabStop_.assert_called_once_with(tab)
      assert tab_stop is tab_stop_
  ```

3. For what it may be worth, there's a non-sequential order in which I write these tests. I start with the test, then fixture comes second. For the test, I start with the action, calling `tab_stops[idx]` in this case. Next I move on to what I want to assert about that. Then I look for variables I need (flake8 built into my editor (vim) highlights those for me), and I have them come from the fixture, in the order they're needed. So middle, bottom, top order, in which the first step is easy and each step leads to the next.

  On the fixture, I start with the return statement, exactly matching the LHS of the assignment in the test. Then I work out how to get those items one by one. It's remarkably mechanical once you get the hang of it; the same patterns over and over.

Often I was encountering the following problem.
Looking at the UI, a project had, for example, 18 blacklisted findings. Upon migrating the blacklist the result might have said something like: 6/34 findings where successfully blacklisted.
Now I wasn't sure ift there was actually a problem or not. When I removed the filtering of already blacklisted findings it said: 18/34 findings where blacklisted. Upon closer inspection I found out, that the other 16 findings did no longer exist on any project there.
So I kept it, because if the numbers where actually similar it is easier to determine if the migration was successful or not. We are not really running into any performance bottleneck here, so in the end it doesn't really matter.

INFO

basically just "create a substring not in original string" so that we can use the same logic of string.format to see if we will alter the `--title` string given by the user without creating key errors. 

we can't just do `if args.title != args.title.format(title="different")` because if `{author}` is in args.title then we get a key error. So I create these tokens that are defined in a way that they cannot possibly be substrings of `args.title`, and we're still able to track which format code was replaced.

If those substring tokens appear in arg_title_formatted, then I know that args.title.format put them there. and I can compare what token is in args_title_formatted to determine which format code was in the original string.

a simpler implementation would be something like `if "{author}" in args.title:`, but "what if the user wants to put {{author}} in the title?". I had flashbacks to when I implemented custom quotes in #623 and everyone hated it. This implementation uses no regex's and only uses the same exact string.format logic to detect if string.format will string.format the string.
